mission.title: Install and Configure Grafana on Kubernetes
mission.description: Grafana is the open-source dashboard and observability platform that lets teams query, visualize, alert on, and explore metrics, logs, and traces from data sources such as Prometheus, Loki, Thanos, Mimir, Tempo, Elasticsearch, and many more. This mission installs Grafana via the official Helm chart and walks through accessing the UI, retrieving the bootstrap admin password, and the production hardening users typically apply (persistent storage, ingress, externalized admin credentials, datasource provisioning).

steps.title: Add the Grafana Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the official Grafana Helm chart repository to your local Helm configuration.
```bash
helm repo add grafana https://grafana.github.io/helm-charts
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install Grafana using Helm
steps.description:
Install Grafana into a new namespace called 'grafana'. Persistence is disabled by default in the chart, which is fine for a fresh install. The next step turns it on for any environment where dashboard state must survive a pod restart.
```bash
helm install grafana grafana/grafana --version 10.5.15 --namespace grafana --create-namespace
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check that the Grafana pod is running and the Service is created.
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace grafana
kubectl get svc --namespace grafana
```

steps.title: Enable persistence (recommended for any non-throwaway install)
steps.description:
Without persistence, dashboards, datasources, and the SQLite database for users and folders are lost on every pod restart. Enable persistence so a PVC backs the Grafana data directory.
```bash
helm upgrade grafana grafana/grafana --version 10.5.15 \
  --namespace grafana \
  --reuse-values \
  --set persistence.enabled=true \
  --set persistence.size=10Gi
```
A default StorageClass must exist on the cluster for the PVC to bind. On clusters with multiple StorageClasses, set 'persistence.storageClassName' to pick one explicitly.

steps.title: Retrieve the bootstrap admin password
steps.description:
The chart auto-generates an admin password on first install and stores it in a Secret. Read it once and rotate it immediately for any environment that is reachable beyond your laptop.
```bash
kubectl get secret grafana --namespace grafana -o jsonpath='{.data.admin-password}' | base64 -d ; echo
```
For production, do not rely on the auto-generated password. Pre-create a Secret with your own admin credentials and point the chart at it via 'admin.existingSecret', or set 'adminPassword' as a chart value pulled from your secret manager.

steps.title: Access the Grafana UI
steps.description:
Set up port forwarding to reach the Grafana UI from your local machine.
```bash
kubectl port-forward svc/grafana --namespace grafana 3000:80
```
Open http://localhost:3000 in a browser. Log in with username 'admin' and the password you retrieved in the previous step.

steps.title: Configure resource limits
steps.description:
Set resource limits on the Grafana deployment to match your dashboard load and concurrent-user count.
```bash
helm upgrade grafana grafana/grafana --version 10.5.15 \
  --namespace grafana \
  --reuse-values \
  --set resources.requests.cpu=100m \
  --set resources.requests.memory=256Mi \
  --set resources.limits.cpu=500m \
  --set resources.limits.memory=512Mi
```

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the Grafana release from the cluster.
```bash
helm uninstall grafana --namespace grafana
```

uninstall.title: Clean up persistent resources
uninstall.description:
If persistence was enabled, the PVC is retained by default after uninstall. Delete it explicitly when you want the storage released.
```bash
kubectl delete pvc --all --namespace grafana
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Delete the 'grafana' namespace and verify it has been removed.
```bash
kubectl delete namespace grafana
kubectl get namespaces
```

upgrade.title: Backup current values and SQLite database
upgrade.description:
Export the current Helm values before upgrading. If persistence is enabled, also snapshot the SQLite database that backs Grafana's users, folders, and dashboard metadata.
```bash
helm get values grafana --namespace grafana > grafana-backup.yaml
kubectl exec -n grafana deploy/grafana -c grafana -- sqlite3 /var/lib/grafana/grafana.db .dump > grafana-db-backup.sql
```

upgrade.title: Update the Helm repository
upgrade.description:
Refresh the Grafana Helm repository to pick up the latest chart.
```bash
helm repo update
helm search repo grafana/grafana --versions | head -5
```

upgrade.title: Upgrade Grafana
upgrade.description:
Upgrade the release to the desired chart version. Always review the chart and Grafana release notes for any breaking changes before upgrading production.
```bash
helm upgrade grafana grafana/grafana --namespace grafana --version 10.5.15 --reuse-values
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
Check that the Grafana deployment rolled out and is running the new image.
```bash
kubectl rollout status deploy/grafana -n grafana
kubectl get pods -n grafana -o jsonpath='{.items[*].spec.containers[*].image}'
```

troubleshooting.title: Pod stuck in Pending after enabling persistence
troubleshooting.description:
If the Grafana pod stays Pending after 'persistence.enabled=true' is set, the PVC has nothing to bind to. Confirm a default StorageClass exists or that 'persistence.storageClassName' points at a real one.
```bash
kubectl get pvc -n grafana
kubectl get storageclass
kubectl describe pod -n grafana -l app.kubernetes.io/name=grafana | tail -20
```

troubleshooting.title: Cannot log in with the bootstrap admin password
troubleshooting.description:
The chart only generates a password on the first install. If the Secret was deleted or recreated, the persisted SQLite database may have a different password. Check what is actually in the Secret and what is in the database file.
```bash
kubectl get secret grafana -n grafana -o jsonpath='{.data.admin-password}' | base64 -d ; echo
kubectl exec -n grafana deploy/grafana -c grafana -- grafana-cli admin reset-admin-password NEW_PASSWORD
```

troubleshooting.title: Datasource added in the UI disappears after restart
troubleshooting.description:
Datasources added through the UI are written to the SQLite database. If persistence is disabled, the database lives on the pod's emptyDir and is lost on restart. Either enable persistence as in the install steps, or provision datasources as code through 'datasources.datasources.yaml' chart values so they are reapplied on each rollout.
```bash
kubectl get pvc -n grafana
helm get values grafana --namespace grafana | grep -A20 datasources
```

troubleshooting.title: OOMKilled under dashboard load
troubleshooting.description:
Grafana renders dashboards in-memory. Heavy dashboards or many concurrent users can push the pod past its memory limit. Check current usage and bump the limit.
```bash
kubectl top pods --namespace grafana
kubectl describe pod -n grafana -l app.kubernetes.io/name=grafana | grep -A4 Limits
```

troubleshooting.title: UI not reachable through ingress
troubleshooting.description:
If 'ingress.enabled=true' but the UI does not load, check that the ingress controller is installed, the Ingress resource is admitted, and the host header matches.
```bash
kubectl get ingress -n grafana
kubectl describe ingress -n grafana grafana
```

troubleshooting.title: Plugin install fails on pod startup
troubleshooting.description:
If you set 'plugins:' in chart values, Grafana installs them on container start by reaching out to grafana.com. Pods that cannot egress to the internet will fail at this step. Either pre-bake plugins into a custom image, or vendor them via 'extraInitContainers' from an internal mirror.
```bash
kubectl logs -n grafana -l app.kubernetes.io/name=grafana --tail=200
```

resolution.summary: A successful install of Grafana shows the grafana pod Running in the 'grafana' namespace, a Service on port 80 forwarding to container port 3000, and the UI reachable on http://localhost:3000 after port-forward. With persistence enabled the SQLite database survives restarts. Production deployments should additionally set externalized admin credentials, an Ingress with TLS, and provisioned datasources/dashboards from chart values rather than UI-created state.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add grafana https://grafana.github.io/helm-charts
helm repo update

helm upgrade grafana grafana/grafana --version 10.5.15 \
  --namespace grafana --reuse-values \
  --set persistence.enabled=true --set persistence.size=10Gi
kubectl get secret grafana --namespace grafana -o jsonpath='{.data.admin-password}' | base64 -d ; echo

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running and have Helm and kubectl installed on your local machine. A default StorageClass is needed for the PVC if persistence is enabled (the recommended path beyond a throwaway demo).
